revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:海柘榴市 (大和国)]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tsubaichi''' (海柘榴市) was an ancient [[:en:Market (place)|marketplace]] located in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], Japan. Over time, it became a [[Shukuba|post station]] from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|qq=|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|qq=|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|qq=|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|qq=|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|qq=|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]] gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|qq=|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==
* {{Cite book|author=Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|title=Sakurai City History|volume=Volume 1|year=1979|publisher=Sakurai City}}
* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

== Related Articles ==
* [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]
* [[:en:Commandery (China)|Kōri (administrative division)]]

It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:海柘榴市 (大和国)]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tsubaichi''' (海柘榴市) was an ancient [[:en:Market (place)|marketplace]] located in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], Japan. Over time, it became a [[Shukuba|post station]] from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]] gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

Over time, it became a [[Shukuba|post station]] from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]] gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== Further Reading ==

* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]] gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==
* {{Cite book|author=Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|title=Sakurai City History|volume=Volume 1|year=1979|publisher=Sakurai City}}
* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

* [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]
* [[:en:Commandery (China)|Kōri (administrative division)]]

It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

Over time, it became a [[Shukuba|post station]] from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]] gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==
* {{Cite book|author=Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|title=Sakurai City History|volume=Volume 1|year=1979|publisher=Sakurai City}}
* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

== Related Articles ==
* [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]
* [[:en:Commandery (China)|Kōri (administrative division)]]

It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]] gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== Further Reading ==

* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

'''Tsubaichi''' (海柘榴市) was an ancient [[:en:Market (place)|marketplace]] located in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]], Japan. Over time, it became a [[Shukuba|post station]] from the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]] gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==

It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]] gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== References ==

== Further Reading ==

* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]] gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]], Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''[[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]]'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''[[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]]''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for [[:en:Utagaki|Utagaki]], a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' account of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Buretsu|Buretsu]], detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for [[:en:Hase-dera|Hase-dera (Nara)]] pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In [[:en:The Tale of Genji|The Tale of Genji]], it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==
* {{Cite book|author=Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|title=Sakurai City History|volume=Volume 1|year=1979|publisher=Sakurai City}}
* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

== Related Articles ==
* [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]
* [[:en:Commandery (China)|Kōri (administrative division)]]

Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the [[:en:Genki (era)|Genki (era)]] era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

Over time, it became a [[Shukuba|post station]] from the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and {{ill|Utagaki|en|Utagaki|WD=Q610915|lt=Utagaki}} gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}}, Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for {{ill|Hase-dera|en|Hase-dera|WD=Q1457787|lt=Hase-dera (Nara)}} pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''{{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book}}'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''{{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji}}''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the {{ill|Genki (era)|en|Genki (era)|WD=Q1145064|lt=Genki (era)}} era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for {{ill|Utagaki|en|Utagaki|WD=Q610915|lt=Utagaki}}, a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' account of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Buretsu}}, detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for {{ill|Hase-dera|en|Hase-dera|WD=Q1457787|lt=Hase-dera (Nara)}} pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In {{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji}}, it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==
* {{Cite book|author=Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|title=Sakurai City History|volume=Volume 1|year=1979|publisher=Sakurai City}}
* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}
* {{ill|Commandery (China)|en|Commandery (China)|WD=Q517267|lt=Kōri (administrative division)}}

It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}}, though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and {{ill|Utagaki|en|Utagaki|WD=Q610915|lt=Utagaki}} gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}}, Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for {{ill|Hase-dera|en|Hase-dera|WD=Q1457787|lt=Hase-dera (Nara)}} pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''{{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book}}'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''{{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji}}''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hasegawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the {{ill|Genki (era)|en|Genki (era)|WD=Q1145064|lt=Genki (era)}} era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for {{ill|Utagaki|en|Utagaki|WD=Q610915|lt=Utagaki}}, a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' account of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Buretsu}}, detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for {{ill|Hase-dera|en|Hase-dera|WD=Q1457787|lt=Hase-dera (Nara)}} pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In {{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji}}, it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==
* {{Cite book|author=Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|title=Sakurai City History|volume=Volume 1|year=1979|publisher=Sakurai City}}
* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

== Related Articles ==
* {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}
* {{ill|Commandery (China)|en|Commandery (China)|WD=Q517267|lt=Kōri (administrative division)}}

It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

The area was a major transportation hub from the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, intersecting the [[Hase River (Nara)|Hase River]], Teragawa waterways, and major roads like the {{ill|Yoko Ōji (Nara)|ja|横大路 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoko Ōji (Nara)|1=Yoko Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11542431}}, {{ill|Hase Kaidō|ja|初瀬街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase Kaidō|1=Hase Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396532}}, and {{ill|Ise Kaidō|ja|伊勢街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 伊勢参宮街道|1=Ise Sangū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11378967}} running east-west, as well as {{ill|Uetsu Michi|ja|上街道 (上ツ道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uetsu Michi|1=Kami Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11360095}}, {{ill|Abe Yamada Michi|ja|安倍山田道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe Yamada Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe-no-Michi|lt=Yamanobe no Michi|ja|山辺の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanobe no Michi|qq=}} running north-south.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=75–77}}{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} Tsubaichi served as a gateway to capitals like {{ill|Oshikuma Palace|ja|小墾田宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oshikuma Palace|1=Oharida Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11459565}}, hosted {{ill|Ekiya|ja|駅家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekiya|1=umaya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668204}}, and was a place for punishments and {{ill|Utagaki|en|Utagaki|WD=Q610915|lt=Utagaki|1=Utagaki}} gatherings.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}, Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for {{ill|Hase-dera|en|Hase-dera|WD=Q1457787|lt=Hase-dera (Nara)|1=Hase-dera}} pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''{{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}}'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''{{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji|1=The Tale of Genji}}''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}

== Location and "Yaso no Chimata" ==
[[File:海柘榴市の所在地.png|thumb|450px|Location of Tsubaichi and major Asuka-period facilities]]
Tsubaichi is believed to have been located near present-day Kanaya, on the right bank of the {{ill|Hase River (Nara)|ja|初瀬川 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hase River (Nara)|1=Hase River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123408533}} in Sakurai City.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} This area hosts the Tsubaichi Kannon-dō, whose main statues date to the {{ill|Genki (era)|en|Genki (era)|WD=Q1145064|lt=Genki (era)|1=Genki (era)}} era.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} However, some theories suggest Tsubaichi moved here after the Heian period, citing the area's unsuitability as a hub ("chimata").{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Watari|2008|pp=169–172}}

== Market and Origins ==
Tsubaichi was a significant market alongside {{ill|Karuichi|ja|軽市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karuichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Ato Kusaichi, and Ekaiichi during the Asuka period.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=22–32}} While its founding period is uncertain, it may date between the 2nd and 5th centuries.{{sfn|Higuchi|1958|pp=90–94}}

== Utagaki ==
Tsubaichi was a location for {{ill|Utagaki|en|Utagaki|WD=Q610915|lt=Utagaki|1=Utagaki}}, a traditional courtship ritual. It first appears in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' account of Emperor {{ill|Emperor Buretsu|en|Emperor Buretsu|WD=Q334644|lt=Buretsu|1=Emperor Buretsu}}, detailing a romantic exchange between the prince and a local maiden.{{sfn|Watanabe|1980|pp=1–7}}{{sfn|Satō|1974|pp=58–61}}

== Tsubaichi as a Post Station ==
During the Heian period, Tsubaichi thrived as a stop for {{ill|Hase-dera|en|Hase-dera|WD=Q1457787|lt=Hase-dera (Nara)|1=Hase-dera}} pilgrims. ''The Pillow Book'' praises its lively atmosphere.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} In {{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji|1=The Tale of Genji}}, it serves as a meeting place for characters on pilgrimage.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979b|pp=792–793}}

== Decline ==
By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, post station activity shifted to {{ill|Miwa City|ja|三輪市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Sakurai City History Compilation Committee|1979|pp=183–185}}

== Further Reading ==

* {{Cite book|author=Watanabe Shōgo|title=Sacred Origins of Utagaki: The Market and Intersection at Tsubaichi|journal=Kokugakuin University Cultural Studies|year=1980}}

'''Tsubaichi''' (海柘榴市) was an ancient [[:en:Market (place)|marketplace]] located in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}, Japan. Over time, it became a [[Shukuba|post station]] from the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. It is also referred to as '''Usubuichi''', '''Tsubakiichi''', and '''Tunakiichi''', with its name evolving from "Tsubakiichi" to "Tsubaiichi." It is believed to have been situated in the present-day Kanaya area of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, though some theories suggest it may have relocated over time.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}}

After the capital moved to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}, Tsubaichi flourished as a post station for {{ill|Hase-dera|en|Hase-dera|WD=Q1457787|lt=Hase-dera (Nara)|1=Hase-dera}} pilgrims.{{sfn|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|1981|p=430}} It appears in works like ''{{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}}'', diaries of Heian nobility, and ''{{ill|The Tale of Genji|en|The Tale of Genji|WD=Q8269|lt=The Tale of Genji|1=The Tale of Genji}}''.{{sfn|Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Compilation Committee|1990|pp=728–729}}
